Title: Array
Description: A collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations, accessible by index.
Explanation:
An array is one of the most fundamental data structures in computer science. It stores elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations, which allows direct access to any element using its index in O(1) time. Arrays have a fixed size once created, meaning you must know the number of elements in advance.

Arrays support four core operations:
- **Create**: Allocate a block of memory for N elements.
- **Indexing (Access)**: Read or retrieve an element at a specific position using its index. This is O(1).
- **Search**: Scan through the array to find an element by value. This is O(n) for unsorted arrays.
- **Update**: Overwrite the value at a specific index. This is O(1).

Because of their simplicity and cache-friendly memory layout, arrays are the building block for many other data structures such as stacks, queues, and heaps.
